channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: U.S. Strikes Iran and Reimposes Sanctions After Tanker Attacks
item.description: The military operation came hours after the U.S. Treasury revoked a waiver allowing global sales of Iranian oil.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Negotiations between Iran and the United States have been paused until after multiday funeral ceremonies in Tehran for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the supreme leader who was killed on the first day of the war.

item.title: As Israel Loses Support in the U.S., Rahm Emanuel Criticizes Netanyahu
item.description: The former Chicago mayor, a Democrat exploring a 2028 presidential run, is in Tel Aviv calling for an end to unconditional U.S. support of Israel.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rahm Emanuel, the former Chicago mayor and White House chief of staff, is trying to chart a course between the anti-Israel left and the pro-Israel right.

item.title: Democrats Clash Over a Replacement for Platner
item.description: Also, U.S. forces carry out strikes in Iran. Here’s the latest at the end of Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: New Rules Require Election Changes for States to Get Terrorism Grants
item.description: Federal officials said they would withhold some money unless states pursue paper ballot systems, verify citizenship and conduct costly audits.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance, Nick Corasaniti and Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times
item.media:description: Courts have already blocked similar efforts by the administration to withhold funding, saying the Constitution does not grant the executive branch power over elections.

item.title: Haley Stevens Goes on Attack Against Abdul El-Sayed at Michigan Senate Debate
item.description: At their first one-on-one debate in a top Senate race, Representative Haley Stevens accused her progressive rival, Dr. Abdul El-Sayed, of being too focused on publicity as he called her a tool of corporate interests.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Bryan Esler/WOOD TV8
item.media:description: Dr. Abdul El-Sayed and Representative Haley Stevens met on Tuesday night in Grand Rapids for their first Democratic primary debate since a third candidate dropped out.

item.title: Justice Department Threatens Top Election Officials Over Noncitizen Voting
item.description: The letters come amid President Trump’s effort to tighten election rules to prevent a problem that doesn’t exist: widespread noncitizen voting.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Andrew Harnik/Getty Images
item.media:description: Letters from Harmeet Dhillon, who runs the Justice Department’s civil rights division, to all 50 states and the District of Columbia threatened prosecution of top election officials if votes by noncitizens were counted in upcoming elections.

item.title: Judge Quashes Justice Dept. Subpoena in 2020 Election Inquiry
item.description: The ruling cast serious doubts on the department’s efforts to reinvestigate the race, finding that it was too late to bring criminal charges in the case.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the F.B.I. outside of the Fulton County elections office in Georgia this year.

item.title: ‘Beams Started Bending’: Midtown Manhattan Building Sags Dangerously
item.description: Offices and hotels around East 42nd Street were evacuated after structural damage affected an office conversion project. Traffic was halted in the area. No injuries were reported.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Southall and Mihir Zaveri
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: The partial collapse of a 37-story office building being converted into housing on East 42nd Street disrupted Midtown Manhattan on Tuesday.

item.title: Midtown Evacuations Scramble Plans for Hotel Guests
item.description: At least two hotels near the unstable building were evacuated, stranding travelers and forcing guests to clamber for alternative accommodation.
item.dc:creator: Ryley Ober
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sansin Tokyay, right, rolling her luggage up Third Avenue. She was forced to evacuate the Westin New York Grand Central on East 42nd Street.

item.title: What to Know About the ‘Frozen Zone’ in Midtown Manhattan
item.description: A building at risk of partial collapse prompted the city to restrict pedestrian and vehicle traffic in the area. Some buses were diverted or delayed, but subway lines remained uninterrupted.
item.dc:creator: Ed Shanahan
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Snarled traffic in Manhattan after the police cordoned off an area of Midtown East on Tuesday.

item.title: Top Maine Democrat Says Platner Will Have ‘No Role’ in Picking Next Nominee
item.description: A top state party official said that Graham Platner’s team had “repeatedly” tried to “put their thumb on the scale,” but he would not influence how the party chose a new Senate candidate.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner has faced widespread calls from top Democrats to end his Senate campaign after a woman accused him of rape.

item.title: These Maine Democrats Might Replace Graham Platner in the Senate Race
item.description: Mr. Platner has not yet withdrawn from the race after a rape allegation, but Democrats in the state and nationally are already exploring potential alternatives.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Cj Gunther/Getty Images
item.media:description: A wide range of Democrats, including many former allies, have called on Graham Platner to end his campaign for Senate in Maine.

item.title: Marine Le Pen’s Court Battle Is Latest Clash Between Populists and Watchdogs
item.description: The far-right leader’s battle with the French judiciary evokes standoffs across the populist world, including ones with President Trump and Nigel Farage.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Dimitar Dilkoff/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Marine Le Pen leaving court in Paris on Tuesday.

item.title: Le Pen Says She Will Run for Presidency After Court Lifts Ban
item.description: An appeals court confirmed Marine Le Pen’s embezzlement conviction, but shortened a ban on her eligibility for elected office. That means the French far-right leader can run for president in 2027.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Tom Nicholson/Reuters
item.media:description: Marine Le Pen meeting with supporters at a country fair in Liévin, France, on Saturday.

item.title: The World Cup Stood as a Break From U.S. Politics. Then Trump Got Involved.
item.description: The president’s call to the head of FIFA underscores that he has yet to find an area of American, or even global, life where he is unwilling to assert himself.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump accepting the newly created “FIFA Peace Prize” from Gianni Infantino, the FIFA president, last year. Mr. Trump called Mr. Infantino to protest an American player’s suspension.

item.title: ICE Officer Kills a Mexican Man in Houston
item.description: The agency said the man was an “illegal alien” who had tried to run over the agent, though no evidence was immediately offered.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Jacob Lujan/Houston Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The Houston Fire Department said the man was shot in the abdomen.

item.title: Judge Orders D.H.S. to Restore 4 States’ Access to Citizenship Data
item.description: The ruling, based on an agreement the Trump administration signed with Florida last year, contradicted an earlier order by a judge in Washington that required the access be suspended.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and David Ovalle
item.media:credit: Zack Wittman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Election workers counting ballots in Largo, Fla., in 2024. An order by a federal judge in Florida on Tuesday allowed bulk searches of a federal citizenship database by state officials to check voter eligibility.

item.title: Yale Law School Fights to Stop Trump Deal
item.description: The university has been in talks to resolve investigations into its admissions practices. The law school’s dean and some faculty have argued a deal could compromise its independence.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder and Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Students at Yale Law School have also criticized the university’s efforts to seek a deal with the Trump administration.

item.title: Andrea Shaw, Plaintiff in Anti-Vaccine Suit, Is Charged With Murder of Her Twins
item.description: A lawsuit filed by Children’s Health Defense, an organization founded by Robert F. Kennedy Jr., claims that vaccines caused the twins’ deaths. But a grand jury in Idaho has indicted her on charges of suffocating them.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Bonnie Cash/Reuters
item.media:description: Children’s Health Defense’s chief executive said the group planned to stand by Andrea Renee Shaw’s claim that vaccines caused the toddlers’ deaths.

item.title: Suspect Interacted With Charlie Kirk’s Group Before Killing, Prosecutors Say
item.description: In a hearing, prosecutors used videos to trace the steps of the man they accuse of assassinating Mr. Kirk, while defense lawyers tried to poke holes in DNA evidence.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs and Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Trent Nelson
item.media:description: Sgt. David Hull, a Utah law enforcement officer who was one of the lead investigators into Charlie Kirk’s killing, during a hearing in Provo, Utah, on Tuesday.

item.title: The Disappearing Las Vegas Buffets Hold a Mirror to the American Soul
item.description: As a once-grand American tradition enters its twilight, our critic journeys to the desert to find out what it all means.
item.dc:creator: Tejal Rao
item.media:credit: Roger Kisby for The New York Times

item.title: Estonia Won the War on Fentanyl. What Came Next Was Even Worse.
item.description: By 2018, fentanyl overdoses in Estonia had plummeted. But powerful new drugs are appearing fast, with the authorities racing to respond.
item.dc:creator: Azam Ahmed and Meridith Kohut
item.media:description: Long before fentanyl swept American streets, Estonia battled the drug — and won.

item.title: The 39-ish Most Stylish People of 2026, So Far
item.description: On red carpets, on set, onstage and even up in space, they captured our attention with their looks.
item.dc:creator: The Styles Desk
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Democratic Socialists Are on the Rise. We’ve Seen This Movie Before.
item.description: Mainstream party leaders can’t dodge a fight with their left.
item.dc:creator: Bret Stephens
item.media:credit: Will Matsuda for The New York Times

item.title: JD Vance Is Worried
item.description: The vice president’s theology comes in book form.
item.dc:creator: Carlos Lozada
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Communion: Finding My Way Back to Faith (Book)
item.media:credit: Paola Chapdelaine for The New York Times

item.title: Why Are Berries Everywhere, in Every Season? Driscoll’s.
item.description: The California giant has helped turn a local, seasonal treat into a worldwide refrigerator staple and marketing juggernaut.
item.dc:creator: Julia Moskin
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times
